content.problemStatement:
A **hypergraph** is a generalization of a graph, where an edge can connect any number of vertices. A k-**uniform hypergraph** is a hypergraph such that all its hyperedges have size k. For more information, see Wikipedia.



Let's call a particular hypergraph a **hypertree** if it is connected (that is, you can move from any vertex to any other vertex using only its hyperedges) and removing any of its hyperedges makes the hypergraph disconnected (note that this definition of hypertrees differs from the standard one).



Given just one integer **N**, find the number of 3-uniform hypertrees on **N** vertices. Two 3-uniform hypertrees are considered different if a hyperedge (u, v, w) exists such that it is present in exactly one of these hypertrees (note that the order of vertices in the hyperedge doesn't matter, and neither does the order of hyperedges in the hypertree).
content.inputFormat: First line contains T, the number of test cases. Each test case contains a single integer N.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the number of 3-uniform hypertrees on N vertices.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The number of 3-uniform hypertrees on 3, 4, 5, and 8 vertices, respectively.
There is only one 3-uniform hypertree on 3 vertices.
There are six 3-uniform hypertrees on 4 vertices.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a recursive approach to generate all possible 3-uniform hypertrees on N vertices. We start by choosing 3 vertices to form the first hyperedge, then recursively add more hyperedges to the existing hypertree. We need to ensure that the hypertree remains connected and that removing any hyperedge makes the hypertree disconnected.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N*(N-1)*(N-2)/6) — the dominant structure is the recursive call stack.

code.Python3:

def count_hypertrees(N):

    return count

T = int(sys.stdin.readline())

for t in range(T):
    N = int(sys.stdin.readline())

    count = count_hypertrees(N)
    print(count)
